title: Print all root-to-leaf paths
description: Print all paths from the root node to every leaf node in the binary tree

examples.explanation:
Start at root node 1: path=[1]. Node 1 is not a leaf (has children 2 and 3).
Recurse left to node 2: path=[1,2]. Node 2 is a leaf (no children). Add copy of path to result: result=[[1,2]]. Backtrack: remove 2, path=[1].
Recurse right to node 3: path=[1,3]. Node 3 is a leaf. Add copy: result=[[1,2], [1,3]]. Backtrack: remove 3, path=[1].
Backtrack from root: remove 1, path=[]. Return result.

examples.input: Tree: [1,2,3,4,5] (root=1, left=2 with left=4, right=3 with right=5)
examples.explanation:
Start at root 1: path=[1]. Not a leaf.
Recurse left to node 2: path=[1,2]. Not a leaf (has left child 4).
Recurse left to node 4: path=[1,2,4]. Leaf node. Add copy: result=[[1,2,4]]. Backtrack: remove 4, path=[1,2].
Node 2 has no right child, so backtrack: remove 2, path=[1].
Recurse right to node 3: path=[1,3]. Not a leaf (has right child 5).
Recurse right to node 5: path=[1,3,5]. Leaf node. Add copy: result=[[1,2,4], [1,3,5]]. Backtrack: remove 5, path=[1,3].
Backtrack: remove 3, path=[1].
Backtrack: remove 1, path=[]. Return result.

approaches.name: Approach 1: Brute Force Recursive DFS
approaches.summary: Use depth-first search (DFS) to traverse the tree recursively, building a path list as you go. When a leaf node is reached, print or store the current path, then backtrack by removing the last node to explore other branches.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(h) where h is the height of the tree (due to recursion stack and path storage)

ladders.title: Full Problem — Print All Root-to-Leaf Paths Using Recursive DFS
ladders.desc: Complete solution to print all paths from root to every leaf node in a binary tree using recursive DFS with backtracking.
ladders.explanation:
This problem requires traversing a binary tree to find all paths from the root node to every leaf node. A leaf node is defined as a node with no children (both left and right are null). The brute force recursive DFS approach works by performing a depth-first traversal while maintaining a current path list. As we traverse down from the root, we add each visited node's value to the path. When we reach a leaf node (both children are null), we have completed a valid root-to-leaf path, so we record it (e.g., print or store). After exploring a node's subtree, we backtrack by removing the last added node from the path to explore other branches. This ensures all possible paths are covered.

Intuition: The tree structure naturally lends itself to recursion—each node can be seen as the root of its own subtree. By recursively exploring left and right children while tracking the path, we systematically visit every node. Backtracking is crucial because the path must be shared across recursive calls; removing the last node after returning from a subtree allows the same path list to be reused for other branches, saving memory.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Define a recursive function dfs(node, path, result):
   - If node is null, return.
   - Append node.value to path.
   - If node is a leaf (both left and right are null):
        * Add a copy of path to result (since path will be modified later).
   - Else:
        * Recursively call dfs(node.left, path, result).
        * Recursively call dfs(node.right, path, result).
   - Remove the last element from path (backtrack).
2. Initialize an empty result list and call dfs(root, [], result).
3. Return result.

Time complexity: O(n), where n is the number of nodes, because each node is visited exactly once.
Space complexity: O(h), where h is the height of the tree, due to the recursion stack and the path list (which stores up to h elements at any time). In the worst case (skewed tree), h = n, so O(n).

Connection to L1: This full solution relies on the key subroutine of DFS traversal with backtracking, which is broken down in L1.

Common mistakes:
- Forgetting to backtrack: This leads to paths accumulating incorrect nodes.
- Not copying the path when storing: Since path is mutable, storing it directly causes all entries in result to reference the same list, which gets modified.
- Missing base case for null nodes: Can cause errors on empty trees or when reaching null children.

Real code pattern (Python-style pseudocode):
def print_paths(root):
    def dfs(node, path, result):
        if not node:
            return
        path.append(node.val)
        if not node.left and not node.right:
            result.append(path.copy())
        else:
            dfs(node.left, path, result)
            dfs(node.right, path, result)
        path.pop()
    result = []
    dfs(root, [], result)
    return result

examples.trace:
Start at root node 1: path=[1]. Node 1 is not a leaf (has children 2 and 3).
Recurse left to node 2: path=[1,2]. Node 2 is a leaf (no children). Add copy of path to result: result=[[1,2]]. Backtrack: remove 2, path=[1].
Recurse right to node 3: path=[1,3]. Node 3 is a leaf. Add copy: result=[[1,2], [1,3]]. Backtrack: remove 3, path=[1].
Backtrack from root: remove 1, path=[]. Return result.

Recurse left to node 2: path=[1,2]. Not a leaf (has left child 4).
Recurse left to node 4: path=[1,2,4]. Leaf node. Add copy: result=[[1,2,4]]. Backtrack: remove 4, path=[1,2].
Node 2 has no right child, so backtrack: remove 2, path=[1].
Recurse right to node 3: path=[1,3]. Not a leaf (has right child 5).
Recurse right to node 5: path=[1,3,5]. Leaf node. Add copy: result=[[1,2,4], [1,3,5]]. Backtrack: remove 5, path=[1,3].
Backtrack: remove 3, path=[1].
Backtrack: remove 1, path=[]. Return result.

Tree: [1,2,3,null,4,5,null] (root=1, left=2 with right=4, right=3 with left=5)

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DFS Traversal with Backtracking for Path Building
ladders.desc: Implement a recursive DFS function that traverses a binary tree, builds a path, and backtracks after exploring subtrees.
ladders.explanation:
This sub-routine is the core of the full problem: a depth-first search (DFS) function that recursively visits nodes in a binary tree while maintaining a current path. The function takes a node, a path list (which accumulates node values from root to current node), and a result list. It adds the current node's value to the path, then checks if the node is a leaf (no children). If it is a leaf, the current path is a complete root-to-leaf path, so a copy is added to the result. Otherwise, it recursively calls itself on the left and right children. After exploring both children (or if it's a leaf), it backtracks by removing the last element from the path. This backtracking is essential to reuse the same path list for different branches, ensuring memory efficiency.

Intuition: DFS explores as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking. By tracking the path during traversal, we capture the sequence of nodes from root to current position. Backtracking mimics the natural "undo" operation when returning from a recursive call, allowing the path to be correctly shared across the recursion tree.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. Base case: If node is null, return (no action).
2. Append node.value to path.
3. If node.left is null and node.right is null (leaf node):
   - Add a copy of path to result (to avoid mutation issues).
4. Else:
   - Recursively call function on node.left with same path and result.
   - Recursively call function on node.right with same path and result.
5. Remove last element from path (backtrack).

Time complexity: O(n) for visiting all nodes.
Space complexity: O(h) for recursion stack and path storage.

Connection to L0: This sub-routine is called within the full solution to handle the traversal and path recording.

Common mistakes:
- Not copying path when storing in result: This leads to all result entries pointing to the same list, which gets modified during backtracking.
- Forgetting to backtrack: Causes paths to include nodes from previously explored branches.
- Incorrect leaf check: Must check both children are null, not just one.

Real code pattern (Python-style pseudocode):
def dfs(node, path, result):
    if node is None:
        return
    path.append(node.val)
    if node.left is None and node.right is None:
        result.append(path.copy())
    else:
        dfs(node.left, path, result)
        dfs(node.right, path, result)
    path.pop()

examples.input: Tree: [1,2,3], start at root 1 with path=[], result=[]
examples.trace:
Call dfs(1, [], []): path=[1]. Not a leaf.
Call dfs(2, [1], []): path=[1,2]. Leaf node. Add copy: result=[[1,2]]. Backtrack: path=[1].
Call dfs(3, [1], [[1,2]]): path=[1,3]. Leaf node. Add copy: result=[[1,2], [1,3]]. Backtrack: path=[1].
Backtrack from dfs(1): path=[]. Return.

examples.input: Tree: [1,2,null,3], start at root 1 with path=[], result=[]
examples.trace:
Call dfs(1, [], []): path=[1]. Not a leaf.
Call dfs(2, [1], []): path=[1,2]. Not a leaf (has left child 3).
Call dfs(3, [1,2], []): path=[1,2,3]. Leaf node. Add copy: result=[[1,2,3]]. Backtrack: path=[1,2].
Node 2 has no right child, so backtrack: path=[1].
Backtrack from dfs(1): path=[]. Return.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Backtracking in Recursive Tree Traversal
ladders.desc: Understand and implement backtracking by modifying a shared path list during recursive DFS.
ladders.explanation:
Backtracking is a technique used in recursion to undo changes made during exploration so that the same data structure can be reused for different branches. In the context of tree traversal, we maintain a path list that stores node values from the root to the current node. As we recurse down a branch, we append nodes to the path. When we return from a recursive call (after processing a subtree), we remove the last node from the path—this is backtracking. This allows the path to be correctly updated for the next branch without creating new lists at each step, saving memory.

Intuition: Think of the path as a global state that changes as we move through the tree. When we go down to a child, we extend the path. When we come back up, we shorten it to return to the previous state. This mimics a stack: push when going down, pop when going up. Backtracking ensures that after exploring one subtree, the path reflects only the nodes from the root to the current node, ready to explore another subtree.

Step-by-step algorithm for backtracking in DFS:
1. Before recursing into children, append current node to path.
2. After recursing into all children, remove the last node from path.
3. This removal should happen regardless of whether the node is a leaf or internal.

Time complexity: O(1) per node for append/pop operations, but overall O(n) integrated into traversal.
Space complexity: O(h) for the path list.

Connection to L1: Backtracking is a critical part of the DFS sub-routine, enabling path management.

Common mistakes:
- Forgetting to backtrack after recursive calls: Leads to paths containing nodes from multiple branches.
- Backtracking at wrong time: Should happen after processing both children, not before.
- Using separate path lists for each call: This wastes memory; sharing one list with backtracking is efficient.

Real code pattern (Python-style pseudocode):
def backtracking_dfs(node, path):
    if node is None:
        return
    path.append(node.val)  # extend path
    # Process node (e.g., check if leaf)
    backtracking_dfs(node.left, path)
    backtracking_dfs(node.right, path)
    path.pop()  # backtrack

examples.input: Tree: [1,2,3], start at root 1 with path=[]
examples.trace:
Call with node=1: path=[1].
Recurse left to node=2: path=[1,2].
Node 2 is leaf, no children, so return: backtrack, path=[1].
Recurse right to node=3: path=[1,3].
Node 3 is leaf, return: backtrack, path=[1].
Return from node=1: backtrack, path=[]. Done.

examples.input: Tree: [1,2,null,3], start at root 1 with path=[]
examples.trace:
Call with node=1: path=[1].
Recurse left to node=2: path=[1,2].
Recurse left to node=3: path=[1,2,3].
Node 3 is leaf, return: backtrack, path=[1,2].
Node 2 has no right child, return: backtrack, path=[1].
Return from node=1: backtrack, path=[]. Done.

ladders.title: Building Block — Recursive Tree Traversal (DFS)
ladders.desc: Implement depth-first search to visit all nodes in a binary tree recursively.
ladders.explanation:
Recursive tree traversal using depth-first search (DFS) is a fundamental technique for visiting every node in a binary tree. In DFS, we explore as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. For a binary tree, this typically means recursively visiting the left subtree, then the right subtree (pre-order traversal). The recursion naturally follows the tree structure: each node is processed, and then its children are processed recursively.

Intuition: A binary tree is a recursive data structure—each node has a left and right child, which are themselves roots of subtrees. By defining a function that processes a node and then calls itself on the left and right children, we can traverse the entire tree. The base case is when the node is null (no child), at which point the recursion stops.

Step-by-step algorithm for recursive DFS:
1. Define function dfs(node):
   - If node is null, return.
   - Process node (e.g., print value).
   - Recursively call dfs(node.left).
   - Recursively call dfs(node.right).

Time complexity: O(n), where n is the number of nodes, since each node is visited once.
Space complexity: O(h), where h is the height of the tree, due to the recursion stack. In worst case (skewed tree), h = n, so O(n).

Connection to L2: This traversal is the foundation upon which backtracking is added to build paths.

Common mistakes:
- Missing base case: Can lead to infinite recursion or errors when accessing null children.
- Incorrect order of recursive calls: For pre-order, process node before children; other orders (in-order, post-order) are possible but not needed here.
- Not handling empty trees: The root might be null.

Real code pattern (Python-style pseudocode):
def dfs(node):
    if node is None:
        return
    print(node.val)  # process node
    dfs(node.left)
    dfs(node.right)

examples.trace:
Call dfs(1): node not null, print 1.
Call dfs(2): print 2.
Call dfs(left of 2): null, return.
Call dfs(right of 2): null, return.
Return to dfs(1).
Call dfs(3): print 3.
Call dfs(left of 3): null, return.
Call dfs(right of 3): null, return.
Return to dfs(1), done.

Call dfs(1): print 1.
Call dfs(2): print 2.
Call dfs(left of 2): node=3, print 3.
Call dfs(left of 3): null, return.
Call dfs(right of 3): null, return.
Return to dfs(2).
Call dfs(right of 2): null, return.
Return to dfs(1).
Call dfs(right of 1): null, return.
Done.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Identify Leaf Nodes in a Binary Tree
ladders.desc: Check if a given node in a binary tree is a leaf node (has no children).
ladders.explanation:
A leaf node in a binary tree is a node that has no children—both its left and right child pointers are null (or None). Identifying leaf nodes is crucial for problems involving paths or terminal conditions, as they represent endpoints in the tree. This operation is simple but fundamental: given a node, check if its left and right children are absent.

Intuition: In a tree, leaves are the nodes at the "ends" of branches. They have no further descendants, so any traversal that reaches a leaf has completed a path from the root. Checking for leaves involves verifying that both child references are null, which indicates no subtree to explore further.

Step-by-step algorithm:
1. If node is null, return false (a null node is not a leaf; it's an absence of a node).
2. Check if node.left is null and node.right is null.
3. If both are null, return true (leaf).
4. Otherwise, return false (internal node or partially null).

Time complexity: O(1), as it involves constant-time checks.
Space complexity: O(1), no extra space needed.

Connection to L3: In recursive DFS, we use this check to determine when to record a path (at leaves).

Common mistakes:
- Checking only one child: A node with one null child and one non-null child is not a leaf.
- Not handling null node: A null node should not be considered a leaf; it represents no node at all.
- Confusing leaf with root: The root can be a leaf if it has no children (single-node tree).

Real code pattern (Python-style pseudocode):
def is_leaf(node):
    if node is None:
        return False
    return node.left is None and node.right is None

examples.trace: Node is not null. Check left child: null. Check right child: null. Both are null, so return true.
Node is not null. Check left child: not null (node 3). Check right child: null. Not both null, so return false.

testCases.input: Node: value=5, left=null, right=null (in a tree)

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Tree Node Structure and Navigation
ladders.desc: Understand the basic structure of a binary tree node and how to access its children.
ladders.explanation:
A binary tree is a hierarchical data structure composed of nodes. Each node contains a value and references to its left and right children. The topmost node is called the root. Nodes with no children are leaves. This foundation involves understanding how nodes are represented and how to navigate from a node to its children.

Intuition: Think of a binary tree as a family tree: each person (node) can have up to two children (left and right). Starting from the root, you can move to left or right children to explore the tree. The structure is recursive: each child is itself the root of a subtree.

Key concepts:
- Node representation: Typically has attributes: val (value), left (pointer to left child), right (pointer to right child).
- Null children: If a node has no left child, left is null (or None). Same for right.
- Navigation: Given a node, access left child via node.left, right child via node.right.

Step-by-step for navigation:
1. Start at a node (e.g., root).
2. To go to left child: check if node.left is not null, then move to node.left.
3. To go to right child: check if node.right is not null, then move to node.right.
4. If a child is null, it means no node exists in that direction.

Time complexity: O(1) for accessing children.
Space complexity: O(1).

Connection to L4: This foundation is needed to check for leaf nodes by examining left and right children.

Common mistakes:
- Assuming all nodes have two children: In a binary tree, nodes can have 0, 1, or 2 children.
- Not handling null pointers: Accessing node.left or node.right when they are null can cause errors.
- Confusing value with child: The val is data, while left/right are references to other nodes.

Real code pattern (Python-style pseudocode):
class TreeNode:
    def __init__(self, val):
        self.val = val
        self.left = None
        self.right = None
# Example: root = TreeNode(1)
# Access: left_child = root.left

examples.trace: Given node with value 1. Check left: node.left is not null, it points to a node with value 2. Check right: node.right is not null, it points to a node with value 3.
Given node with value 2. Check left: node.left is null, so no left child. Check right: node.right is not null, it points to a node with value 4.

approaches.name: Approach 2: Iterative DFS with Stack
approaches.summary: Simulate DFS using an explicit stack to track nodes and their corresponding paths, avoiding recursion. Push root with its path onto the stack, then pop and process each node, adding children with updated paths until all leaves are found.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) in worst case (due to stack storage for paths)

ladders.title: Full Problem — Print All Root-to-Leaf Paths Using Iterative DFS with Stack
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of printing all paths from root to every leaf node using an explicit stack.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires traversing a binary tree to find all paths from the root to leaf nodes. The iterative DFS approach uses an explicit stack to simulate depth-first traversal without recursion. The intuition is to maintain both the current node and the path from the root to that node in the stack. Starting with the root and an empty path, we push each node onto the stack along with its accumulated path. When we pop a node, if it's a leaf (no children), we record the path. Otherwise, we push its children with updated paths. This ensures we explore all branches systematically. The algorithm works because it mimics recursive DFS but with manual stack management, avoiding recursion overhead and potential stack overflow. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize an empty result list and a stack with (root, [root.val]). 2. While stack is not empty: pop (node, path). 3. If node has no left and right children, add path to result. 4. Else, push right child with path + [right.val] if exists, then left child similarly (to maintain left-first order). 5. Return result. Time complexity is O(n) as each node is processed once. Space complexity is O(n) in worst case (skewed tree) due to stack storing paths. Connection to L1: This integrates the stack traversal logic with path accumulation. Common mistakes: forgetting to copy paths before pushing children (leading to shared references), incorrect order of pushing children (should push right then left for left-first DFS), or not handling empty tree. Real code pattern: Use a stack of tuples (node, path_list).

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize stack = [(1, [1])], result = []. Step 2: Pop (1, [1]) - not leaf (has children 2 and 3). Push (3, [1,3]) then (2, [1,2]) onto stack. Stack now = [(3, [1,3]), (2, [1,2])]. Step 3: Pop (2, [1,2]) - leaf (no children). Add [1,2] to result. Result = [[1,2]]. Step 4: Pop (3, [1,3]) - leaf. Add [1,3] to result. Result = [[1,2], [1,3]]. Step 5: Stack empty, return result.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Iterative DFS Traversal with Stack for Binary Tree
ladders.desc: Implement iterative depth-first traversal using a stack to visit all nodes in a binary tree.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on traversing a binary tree iteratively using a stack, which is the core mechanism for the full problem. The intuition is to simulate the recursive DFS call stack manually. We use a stack data structure to keep track of nodes to visit. Starting from the root, we push it onto the stack. Then, while the stack is not empty, we pop a node, process it (e.g., print its value), and push its children onto the stack. To achieve depth-first order (specifically pre-order: root, left, right), we push the right child first, then the left child, so that the left child is processed next due to LIFO behavior. This ensures we go deep into the left subtree before the right. The algorithm: 1. If root is null, return. 2. Initialize stack with root. 3. While stack not empty: pop node, process node, push right child if exists, then push left child if exists. Time complexity is O(n) as each node is pushed and popped once. Space complexity is O(h) where h is tree height, due to stack storage. Connection to L0: This traversal is extended in L0 by adding path tracking. Common mistakes: pushing children in wrong order (left then right gives right-first DFS), not handling null children, or infinite loops if nodes are re-pushed. Real code pattern: Use a list as stack with append() and pop().

examples.trace: Step 1: Stack = [1]. Step 2: Pop 1, print 1. Push 3, then 2. Stack = [3,2]. Step 3: Pop 2, print 2. No children to push. Stack = [3]. Step 4: Pop 3, print 3. No children. Stack empty. Output: 1 2 3.

Step 1: Stack = [1]. Step 2: Pop 1, print 1. Push 3, then 2. Stack = [3,2]. Step 3: Pop 2, print 2. Push 5, then 4. Stack = [3,5,4]. Step 4: Pop 4, print 4. No children. Stack = [3,5]. Step 5: Pop 5, print 5. No children. Stack = [3]. Step 6: Pop 3, print 3. No children. Stack empty. Output: 1 2 4 5 3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining Paths During Tree Traversal
ladders.desc: Track the path from root to current node while traversing a tree iteratively.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves maintaining a list representing the path from the root to the current node during traversal. The intuition is that as we move down the tree, we extend the path by appending the current node's value; when we backtrack (implicitly via stack pops), we need the path to reflect the ancestor chain. In iterative DFS with a stack, we store both the node and its corresponding path as a tuple in the stack. When we pop a node, we have its path ready. To push children, we create a new path by copying the current path and appending the child's value. This avoids mutation issues and ensures each node has its correct path. The key insight is that paths are built incrementally and stored alongside nodes, so we don't need to reconstruct them from scratch. Algorithm: 1. Start with stack containing (root, [root.val]). 2. On pop, use the path directly. 3. For each child, push (child, path + [child.val]). Time complexity for path operations is O(n) overall, as each node's path creation involves copying, but total work is O(n^2) in worst case (skewed tree) due to copying lists; however, in practice, it's manageable. Space complexity is O(n*h) for storing paths. Connection to L1: This adds path tracking to the basic traversal. Common mistakes: not copying paths (using same list reference leads to all paths being identical), appending in wrong order, or inefficient path copying. Real code pattern: Use list concatenation (path + [value]) to create new lists.

ladders.title: Building Block — Stack Data Structure for LIFO Operations
ladders.desc: Implement and use a stack to manage nodes in last-in-first-out order for tree traversal.
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on the stack data structure, which is essential for iterative DFS. A stack follows LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) principle, meaning the last element added is the first one removed. In tree traversal, we use a stack to simulate the call stack of recursive DFS: as we explore deeper, we push nodes onto the stack, and when we backtrack, we pop them. The intuition is that stacks naturally support depth-first exploration because they prioritize the most recently added node, encouraging deep dives before breadth. Operations: push (add to top), pop (remove from top), peek (view top), and isEmpty. In Python, a list can serve as a stack using append() for push and pop() for pop. For tree traversal, we push the root, then repeatedly pop a node, process it, and push its children. To ensure left-first order, push right child before left child. Time complexity for stack operations is O(1) per operation, and overall O(n) for traversal. Space complexity is O(h) for stack storage. Connection to L2: The stack is used to store node-path pairs. Common mistakes: using queue operations (FIFO) by mistake, not checking stack emptiness, or inefficient stack implementations. Real code pattern: stack = []; stack.append(item); item = stack.pop().

examples.trace: Step 1: Stack = []. Step 2: Push 1 -> stack = [1]. Step 3: Push 2 -> stack = [1,2]. Step 4: Push 3 -> stack = [1,2,3]. Step 5: Pop -> returns 3, stack = [1,2]. Step 6: Pop -> returns 2, stack = [1]. Step 7: Pop -> returns 1, stack = [].

examples.input: Push root A with children B,C for traversal
examples.output: Process order: A,B,C if pushing C then B
examples.trace: Step 1: Stack = [A]. Step 2: Pop A, process A. Push C, then B. Stack = [C,B]. Step 3: Pop B, process B. No children to push. Stack = [C]. Step 4: Pop C, process C. No children. Stack empty. Order: A,B,C.

testCases.input: Push nodes in order root, left, right for stack simulation
testCases.expected: Process root, left, right if pushed right then left
Last 50 pushed elements in reverse order

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Tree Node Access and Child Checking
ladders.desc: Access properties of a binary tree node and check for children.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves working with binary tree nodes, which typically have attributes: value (data stored), left (reference to left child), and right (reference to right child). The intuition is that trees are hierarchical structures, and traversal requires accessing nodes and determining their children to decide movement. To check if a node is a leaf, we verify that both left and right children are null (or None). To access children, we use node.left and node.right. In iterative DFS, we push children onto the stack only if they exist. This operation is fundamental because it controls the flow of traversal: we stop at leaves and branch at internal nodes. Algorithm steps: 1. Define a TreeNode class with value, left, right. 2. Given a node, check if node.left is null and node.right is null for leaf condition. 3. Access node.left and node.right to get children. Time complexity is O(1) per access. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used when pushing/popping nodes from the stack. Common mistakes: not handling null nodes (leading to null pointer errors), incorrect leaf check (using OR instead of AND), or assuming children exist without checking. Real code pattern: class TreeNode: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.left = None; self.right = None. Check leaf: if not node.left and not node.right.

examples.output: Not a leaf, children: left=2, right=3
examples.trace: Step 1: Node value = 1. Step 2: Check leaf: node.left is not null (2) and node.right is not null (3), so not leaf. Step 3: Access children: node.left gives node with value 2, node.right gives node with value 3.
Step 1: Node value = 4. Step 2: Check leaf: node.left is null and node.right is null, so leaf. Step 3: No children to access.

testCases.input: Node: val=7, left=Node(8), right=null, check leaf on child 8
testCases.expected: For node 8: leaf if children null

testCases.input: Node: val=9, left=null, right=Node(10), access right child
Node: val=12, left=Node(13), right=Node(14), check both children
Node: val=20, left=null, right=null, is leaf

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Tree Structure and Terminology
ladders.desc: Understand what a binary tree is, including root, leaf, parent, child, and path concepts.
ladders.explanation: This foundation introduces binary trees, a fundamental data structure in computer science. A binary tree is a hierarchical collection of nodes, where each node has at most two children: left and right. The top node is called the root. Nodes with no children are leaves. The connection from one node to another is an edge, and a sequence of nodes from root to a leaf is a path. The intuition is that trees model hierarchical relationships, like family trees or directory structures. In this problem, we focus on root-to-leaf paths, which are sequences starting at the root and ending at a leaf, including all intermediate nodes. Key terms: Root: the starting node. Leaf: node with no children. Parent: node with children. Child: node connected to a parent. Path: ordered list of nodes from root to a node. Understanding these terms is crucial because the problem asks to print all such paths. For example, in a tree with root 1 and leaves 2 and 3, the paths are [1,2] and [1,3]. This concept is simple but essential for grasping the problem's goal. Connection to L4: This terminology is used when accessing nodes and checking children. Common mistakes: confusing root with leaf, misunderstanding path definition, or not recognizing tree hierarchy. Real code pattern: No code here, just conceptual understanding.

examples.trace: Step 1: Identify root: node 1. Step 2: Check leaves: node 2 has no children, so leaf; node 3 has no children, so leaf. Step 3: Path from root to leaf 2: start at 1, go to 2 -> path [1,2]. Step 4: Path to leaf 3: [1,3].

examples.output: Root: A, Leaves: D and C, Paths: A->B->D and A->C
examples.trace: Step 1: Root: A. Step 2: Leaves: D has no children, C has no children. B has child D, so not leaf. Step 3: Path to leaf D: A->B->D -> [A,B,D]. Step 4: Path to leaf C: A->C -> [A,C].

testCases.expected: No root, no leaves, no paths

approaches.name: Approach 3: BFS with Queue for Level-Order
approaches.summary: Use breadth-first search (BFS) with a queue to traverse the tree level by level, storing paths for each node. When a leaf node is encountered, output its path, ensuring all root-to-leaf paths are found in a top-down manner.
approaches.spaceComplexity: O(n) in worst case (due to queue storage for paths)

ladders.title: Full Problem — BFS Queue Path Traversal
ladders.desc: Complete implementation using BFS with queue to store paths and print all root-to-leaf paths.
ladders.explanation: This solution leverages breadth-first search (BFS) to traverse the binary tree level by level, using a queue to manage both nodes and their corresponding paths from the root. The intuition is that BFS naturally explores all nodes in a top-down manner, allowing us to build paths incrementally as we move down the tree. We start by enqueuing the root node along with its path (just the root's value). While the queue is not empty, we dequeue a node and its path. If the node is a leaf (both children are null), we output the current path. Otherwise, for each non-null child, we create a new path by appending the child's value to the current path and enqueue the child with this new path. This ensures that every leaf node is reached with its complete root-to-leaf path stored. The time complexity is O(n) as we visit each node once, and space complexity is O(n) in the worst case due to queue storage of paths (which could be up to n/2 leaf paths in a balanced tree). This connects to L1 by implementing the full BFS traversal logic that uses the path-building subroutine. Common mistakes include not handling null trees, incorrectly updating paths (mutating instead of copying), or forgetting to check for leaf nodes. Real code pattern: Use a queue of tuples (node, path_list).

examples.input: Tree: [1,2,3,null,5] (root=1, left=2, right=3; node 2 has right=5)
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize queue with (root=1, path=[1]). Queue: [(1,[1])]. Step 2: Dequeue (1,[1]). Node 1 is not leaf (has children 2 and 3). Enqueue left child: (2, [1,2]). Enqueue right child: (3, [1,3]). Queue: [(2,[1,2]), (3,[1,3])]. Step 3: Dequeue (2,[1,2]). Node 2 is not leaf (has right child 5). Enqueue right child: (5, [1,2,5]). Queue: [(3,[1,3]), (5,[1,2,5])]. Step 4: Dequeue (3,[1,3]). Node 3 is leaf (no children). Output path [1,3]. Queue: [(5,[1,2,5])]. Step 5: Dequeue (5,[1,2,5]). Node 5 is leaf. Output path [1,2,5]. Queue empty. Done.
Step 1: Initialize queue with (root=1, path=[1]). Queue: [(1,[1])]. Step 2: Dequeue (1,[1]). Node 1 is leaf (no children). Output path [1]. Queue empty. Done.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — BFS Path Builder
ladders.desc: Implement BFS traversal that builds and stores paths for each node in a queue.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on the core BFS mechanism that maintains paths alongside nodes during traversal. The intuition is that by storing the path from the root to each node as we traverse, we can efficiently generate complete paths when reaching leaves. We use a queue data structure to process nodes in level order. Each queue element is a pair: the current node and a list representing the path from the root to that node. Starting from the root with path containing only the root's value, we repeatedly dequeue a pair. If the node has children, for each child, we create a new path by copying the current path and appending the child's value, then enqueue the child with this new path. This ensures paths are built incrementally without mutation issues. Time complexity is O(n) for visiting n nodes, and space complexity is O(n) due to queue storage (up to O(n) paths in worst case). This feeds into L0 by providing the path-building logic that the full problem uses to output leaf paths. Common mistakes include using a single mutable list for all paths (leading to incorrect updates) or not handling null children properly. Real code pattern: queue = deque(); queue.append((root, [root.val])) while queue: node, path = queue.popleft().

examples.output: Paths built: For node 1: [1]; node 2: [1,2]; node 3: [1,3]
examples.trace: Step 1: Enqueue (1,[1]). Queue: [(1,[1])]. Step 2: Dequeue (1,[1]). Node 1 has children 2 and 3. For child 2: new path = [1] + [2] = [1,2]; enqueue (2,[1,2]). For child 3: new path = [1] + [3] = [1,3]; enqueue (3,[1,3]). Queue: [(2,[1,2]), (3,[1,3])]. Step 3: Dequeue (2,[1,2]). Node 2 has no children, keep path [1,2]. Step 4: Dequeue (3,[1,3]). Node 3 has no children, keep path [1,3]. Queue empty.

examples.output: Paths built: For node 1: [1]; node 2: [1,2]; node 3: [1,2,3]
examples.trace: Step 1: Enqueue (1,[1]). Queue: [(1,[1])]. Step 2: Dequeue (1,[1]). Node 1 has left child 2. For child 2: new path = [1] + [2] = [1,2]; enqueue (2,[1,2]). Queue: [(2,[1,2])]. Step 3: Dequeue (2,[1,2]). Node 2 has left child 3. For child 3: new path = [1,2] + [3] = [1,2,3]; enqueue (3,[1,2,3]). Queue: [(3,[1,2,3])]. Step 4: Dequeue (3,[1,2,3]). Node 3 has no children, keep path [1,2,3]. Queue empty.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Level-Order Traversal with Queue
ladders.desc: Perform BFS traversal on a binary tree using a queue to visit nodes level by level.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves traversing a binary tree in breadth-first order, which means visiting all nodes at the current depth before moving to the next level. The intuition is that a queue (First-In-First-Out) perfectly manages this order: we start by enqueuing the root, then repeatedly dequeue a node, process it, and enqueue its children. This ensures nodes are processed in the order they are discovered, level by level. The algorithm: initialize a queue with the root. While the queue is not empty, dequeue a node, visit it (e.g., store its value), and if it has left child, enqueue left; if it has right child, enqueue right. Time complexity is O(n) as each node is enqueued and dequeued once. Space complexity is O(n) in the worst case (e.g., a full binary tree where the last level has ~n/2 nodes). This feeds into L1 by providing the traversal framework that the path builder extends with path storage. Common mistakes include not handling null roots, infinite loops from incorrect queue management, or missing children enqueueing. Real code pattern: queue = deque([root]) while queue: node = queue.popleft() if node.left: queue.append(node.left) if node.right: queue.append(node.right).

examples.trace: Step 1: Enqueue root 1. Queue: [1]. Step 2: Dequeue 1, visit 1. Enqueue left child 2 and right child 3. Queue: [2,3]. Step 3: Dequeue 2, visit 2. No children to enqueue. Queue: [3]. Step 4: Dequeue 3, visit 3. No children. Queue empty. Visited order: 1,2,3.

Step 1: Enqueue root 1. Queue: [1]. Step 2: Dequeue 1, visit 1. Enqueue left child 2 and right child 3. Queue: [2,3]. Step 3: Dequeue 2, visit 2. Enqueue left child 4 and right child 5. Queue: [3,4,5]. Step 4: Dequeue 3, visit 3. No children. Queue: [4,5]. Step 5: Dequeue 4, visit 4. No children. Queue: [5]. Step 6: Dequeue 5, visit 5. No children. Queue empty. Visited order: 1,2,3,4,5.

ladders.title: Building Block — Queue Operations for Tree Nodes
ladders.desc: Use a queue to store and process tree nodes in FIFO order.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves implementing queue operations specifically for handling binary tree nodes. The intuition is that a queue allows us to manage nodes in the order they are discovered, which is essential for level-order traversal. We use enqueue (append) to add nodes to the back of the queue and dequeue (remove from front) to process nodes in the order they were added. For trees, we typically start by enqueuing the root node. Then, in a loop, we dequeue a node, process it (e.g., check its value or children), and enqueue its non-null children. This ensures that all nodes at the current level are processed before their children, maintaining the BFS order. Time complexity for enqueue and dequeue is O(1) per operation, and overall O(n) for n nodes. Space complexity is O(n) for queue storage. This feeds into L2 by providing the queue management that enables level-order traversal. Common mistakes include using a list without efficient deque operations (leading to O(n) dequeue time), not checking for null before enqueueing, or incorrect loop conditions. Real code pattern: from collections import deque; queue = deque(); queue.append(root); while queue: node = queue.popleft().

examples.input: Enqueue root 1, then dequeue and enqueue children 2 and 3
examples.output: Queue states: start [1], after dequeue [], after enqueues [2,3]
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize empty queue. Step 2: Enqueue node 1. Queue: [1]. Step 3: Dequeue: remove node 1 from front. Queue: []. Process node 1. Step 4: Enqueue node 2. Queue: [2]. Step 5: Enqueue node 3. Queue: [2,3]. Done.

examples.input: Queue initially [2,3], dequeue 2 and enqueue its children 4 and 5
examples.output: Queue states: start [2,3], after dequeue [3], after enqueues [3,4,5]
examples.trace: Step 1: Queue: [2,3]. Step 2: Dequeue: remove node 2 from front. Queue: [3]. Process node 2. Step 3: Enqueue node 4. Queue: [3,4]. Step 4: Enqueue node 5. Queue: [3,4,5]. Done.

testCases.expected: Processed node: 13; error or no-op on second dequeue

testCases.input: Enqueue 18, enqueue 19, dequeue, enqueue 20, dequeue, dequeue

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Tree Node Access and Child Check
ladders.desc: Access a binary tree node's value and check for left/right children.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves interacting with a binary tree node structure. In a typical implementation, a tree node has attributes: value (or data), left child pointer, and right child pointer. The intuition is that to traverse a tree, we need to access the node's value to process it and check its children to decide further movement. For example, in BFS, after dequeuing a node, we visit its value and then check if left and right children exist (not null) to enqueue them. This operation is fundamental because it allows us to navigate the tree structure. Time complexity is O(1) per access, and space is O(1). This feeds into L3 by providing the node inspection needed for queue operations. Common mistakes include assuming a child exists without null check (leading to errors), or incorrectly accessing attributes. Real code pattern: class TreeNode: def __init__(self, val): self.val = val; self.left = None; self.right = None. To access: node.val, node.left, node.right.

examples.input: Node with value 10, left child with value 5, right child null
examples.output: Access value: 10; left child exists: yes (value 5); right child exists: no
examples.trace: Step 1: Given node A with val=10, left=node B (val=5), right=None. Step 2: Access node.val -> 10. Step 3: Check node.left -> not None, so left child exists. Step 4: Check node.right -> None, so right child does not exist.

examples.output: Access value: 7; left child exists: no; right child exists: no
examples.trace: Step 1: Given node with val=7, left=None, right=None. Step 2: Access node.val -> 7. Step 3: Check node.left -> None. Step 4: Check node.right -> None.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Tree Structure
ladders.desc: Understand what a binary tree is and its basic properties.
ladders.explanation: A binary tree is a hierarchical data structure composed of nodes, where each node has at most two children: a left child and a right child. The topmost node is called the root. Nodes with no children are called leaves. The intuition is that trees represent hierarchical relationships, like family trees or organizational charts, and binary trees are a specific type where each node branches into at most two directions. This structure allows for efficient searching and traversal algorithms. Key properties: the root is the starting point, each node (except root) has exactly one parent, and paths from root to leaves define sequences. Time and space concepts are not directly applicable here, but understanding this structure is prerequisite for all tree algorithms. This feeds into L4 by providing the context for node access and child checks. Common mistakes include confusing binary trees with binary search trees (which have ordering constraints) or misunderstanding parent-child relationships. Real code pattern: Represent nodes with a class having value, left, and right attributes.

examples.input: A simple binary tree with root value 1, left child 2, right child 3
examples.output: Structure: root=1, left=2, right=3; leaf nodes: 2 and 3
examples.trace: Step 1: Create root node with value 1. Step 2: Attach left child node with value 2 to root. Step 3: Attach right child node with value 3 to root. Step 4: Identify leaves: nodes 2 and 3 have no children, so they are leaves. The tree has one root and two leaves.

examples.input: Binary tree with root 4, left child 5, and left child of 5 is 6
examples.output: Structure: root=4, left=5, right=null; node 5 has left=6, right=null; leaf nodes: 6
examples.trace: Step 1: Create root node with value 4. Step 2: Attach left child node 5 to root. Step 3: Node 5 has left child node 6. Step 4: Identify leaves: node 6 has no children, so it is a leaf. Node 4 has one child (5), node 5 has one child (6).

testCases.input: Tree: root 5, left 6, left of 6 is 7
Tree: root 8, right 9, right of 9 is 10
Tree: root 11, left 12, right 13, left of 12 is 14
Tree: root 15, left 16, right 17, right of 16 is 18
Tree: root 19, left 20, right 21, left of 20 is 22, right of 20 is 23
Tree: root 24, right 25, left of 25 is 26
Tree: root 27, left 28, right 29, left of 28 is 30, right of 28 is 31
Tree: root 32, left 33, right 34, left of 33 is 35, right of 33 is 36, left of 34 is 37
